title: RFE: option to change the events verbose
body:
### Feature request description

Currently, if you use  `events_logger = "journald"` (the default) and have many containers, you'll end up with a journal spammed of (most times) irrelevant podman events. Here's a proof: https://gist.githubusercontent.com/Edu4rdSHL/7484cb674db93fee5905270c1d1df7b1/raw/340bd5d8d26f8dcbecf3d37f1f740df6cc404698/gistfile0.txt

The workaround is to use `file` or `none` as `events_logger`, but you'll lose the ability to `podman container logs -f`.

### Suggest potential solution

Having a way to control what's reported to the journal using a `events_logger_level = debug/info/warn/err/crit` variable, would be awesome.

### Have you considered any alternatives?

_No response_

### Additional context

_No response_

comments.body:
> Having a way to control what's reported to the journal using a events_logger_level = debug/info/warn/err/crit variable, would be awesome.

It is not clear what you mean by that. Simply chaining the syslog level at which we log? I don't see how this would help as we would still write everything.
And if you mean podman should have logic to log only some events then this is not something we can do realistically. We have no knowledge on the importance of a event. Events are there to be consumed by external consumers (e.g. podman-desktop, cockpit, etc...) to refresh there state. Not logging a subset of events will simply break all those tools.




> but you'll lose the ability to podman container logs -f.

You can also switch the container logger to `k8s-file` then this should work again.
But in general this error is overly strict and could be just fixed to not error out. The only reason this was added was because log reading is racy.

So yeah I think the answer is to set the event_logger to file.

> It is not clear what you mean by that. Simply chaining the syslog level at which we log? I don't see how this would help as we would still write everything. And if you mean podman should have logic to log only some events then this is not something we can do realistically. We have no knowledge on the importance of a event. Events are there to be consumed by external consumers (e.g. podman-desktop, cockpit, etc...) to refresh there state. Not logging a subset of events will simply break all those tools.
> 
Hello, thanks for your prompt reply. What I mean basically is: would be good to have a mechanism to filter the events logged to the journal by priority, like what `podman --log-level` does, but that setting seems to no effect on the events log. My main issue with this, is that my journal gets filled with dozens of lines every minute with information that I really don't want/need (what I want is already available on `podman container logs`). But as you said, it isn't realistically possible.

So, I have another question: is there a way to control that log via journald? Journald does have [LogLevelMax=](https://www.freedesktop.org/software/systemd/man/latest/systemd.exec.html#LogLevelMax=), but I'm not sure which unit(s) send these logs, everything I see is that it's being sent by `podman`.

> You can also switch the container logger to `k8s-file` then this should work again. But in general this error is overly strict and could be just fixed to not error out. The only reason this was added was because log reading is racy.
> 
> So yeah I think the answer is to set the event_logger to file.

Thanks.

> Hello, thanks for your prompt reply. What I mean basically is: would be good to have a mechanism to filter the events logged to the journal by priority, like what podman --log-level does, but that setting seems to no effect on the events log. My main issue with this, is that my journal gets filled with dozens of lines every minute with information that I really don't want/need (what I want is already available on podman container logs). But as you said, it isn't realistically possible.

Yeah that will just turn into a huge maintenance mess. There are many external tools that want to consume the events so I don't think podman should filter.



> So, I have another question: is there a way to control that log via journald? Journald does have [LogLevelMax=](https://www.freedesktop.org/software/systemd/man/latest/systemd.exec.html#LogLevelMax=), but I'm not sure which unit(s) send these logs, everything I see is that it's being sent by podman.

Well yes there are ways, my understanding would be that this is cgroup based in systemd. So if you set your entire login session to such log level it would hide the message if you set a high level but that the likely affects all commands.

We already change the journal level for hc events to avoid so much systemd log spam for starting stopping the hc units all the time but we still need the event because users might want it, i.e. https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/20347


So overall if you don't want journald spammed with your messages use the file logger and qalso the k8s-file logger for the container to avoid the `podman logs --follow` problem. Because by default the container stdout/err are written to the journal too so this can generate much more spam.

If you want event written to a file and container logs to journal then we should fix the --follow problem, I suggest to file a new feature request for that if you need it. But so far I don't think the use case for that was there, either people set both to file or both to journal logging so it is not a common use case I suppose.
For anyone wondering, you can disable the events logging if you don't need them by setting `events_logger = "none"` under the `[engine]` section.
